data.title: Why did Circle's stock price plummet after 140 companies joined forces to issue a stablecoin?
data.description:
TL;DR：

· Open Standard announced the launch of its US dollar stablecoin, Open USD (OUSD). Reuters reports that the project brings together more than 140 companies, including payment, financial, technology, and crypto institutions such as Visa, Mastercard, and Coinbase. Open USD is currently in the announcement phase, with the official announcement stating that it will launch within the year.

· Open USD's core design includes zero-fee minting and redemption, no issuance size limit, distribution of reserve returns to partners, and neutral governance. This model contrasts with Circle's business model, which primarily relies on USDC reserve asset returns ...>

Following the announcement of Open USD, CRCL closed at $62.63 on June 30th (US time), a single-day drop of approximately 17.52%. Market focus is on whether Open USD will weaken USDC's distribution advantages and drive stablecoin yields away from issuers and towards distributors such as payment networks, exchanges, wallets, and merchant platforms. Market opinions remain divided. Some believe that Open USD's institutional lineup and yield-sharing mechanism may put pressure on Circle; others argue that consortium stablecoins still face challenges such as a cold start in liquidity, insufficient transaction pairing, governance efficiency, and genuine adoption, making it difficult to directly challenge USDC and USDT in the short term. Open Standard Announces Launch of Open USD On the evening of June 30th, Beijing time, Open Standard announced the launch of its USD stablecoin, Open USD (OUSD). Reuters reported that Open Standard has brought together over 140 companies to issue a stablecoin pegged to the US dollar, Open USD. The stablecoin is currently in the announcement phase, with the official announcement stating it will launch within the year. Open Standard Founding CEO Zach Abrams stated that while existing stablecoins have their advantages, for companies to use stablecoins on a large scale, they need an open, low-cost, high-throughput, widely accessible product that aligns with their own interests. Reuters reports that OpenUSD will allow companies to mint and redeem at no cost, with no limit on issuance size. Profits generated from the OpenUSD reserve assets will also be shared with participants after deducting management fees. The participants cover multiple sectors including payments, banking, technology, and crypto. Reuters reports that the alliance includes companies such as Visa, Mastercard, and Coinbase; CoinDesk reports that Stripe, Coinbase, Mastercard, Visa, and BlackRock are among the project's launch partners. According to a WSJ report, companies such as BlackRock, Google, Coinbase, Visa, Stripe, and Mastercard are involved, and OpenUSD is planned to be offered on Base, Solana, and other networks. It's important to note that the participation list does not equate to completed product integration. A more accurate description is that these institutions have joined the Open Standard or OpenUSD ecosystem and may collaborate on stablecoin issuance, payments, settlements, merchant services, trading, and on-chain applications in the future. The core mechanisms of OpenUSD: zero fees, revenue sharing, and consortium governance. The first mechanism of OpenUSD is zero-fee minting and redemption. Reuters reports that OpenUSD will allow companies to mint and redeem without cost or issuance size restrictions. The design primarily targets enterprise-level payment, settlement, and fund transfer scenarios. The second mechanism is reserve revenue sharing. Reuters reports that the revenue generated by Open USD's reserve assets will be shared with participants after deducting management fees. CoinDesk also reports that Open Standard's Open USD allows partners to retain reserve revenue and eliminates minting fees, a design that challenges Circle's USDC model. The third mechanism is consortium governance. Reuters reports that Open Standard hopes to expand stablecoin usage through open, low-cost, and neutral governance. For payment companies, exchanges, banks, e-commerce platforms, and on-chain applications, this means they are not only stablecoin users but may also become revenue and governance participants. Why is Open USD impacting Circle? Following the release of Open USD, the market's primary focus was on Circle. This is because Circle is the issuer of USDC, one of the most prominent USD stablecoins currently on the market. Reserve revenue constitutes a significant portion of Circle's revenue structure. The revenue-sharing model proposed by Open USD directly corresponds to Circle's core revenue source. Circle's Q1 2026 financial report shows that as of the end of Q1, the circulating supply of USDC was $77 billion, a year-on-year increase of 28%; USDC on-chain transaction volume in Q1 was $21.5 trillion, a year-on-year increase of 263%. In the same quarter, Circle's total revenue and reserve revenue were $694 million, a year-on-year increase of 20%; of which reserve revenue was $653 million, a year-on-year increase of 17%. Circle also disclosed that its total distribution, transaction, and other costs in the first quarter were $407 million, a 17% year-over-year increase, primarily due to increased distribution payments. This data illustrates that Circle's revenue is closely related to the circulating supply of USDC and the returns on its reserve assets. If OpenUSD distributes more of its reserve returns to its partners, the market will reassess the revenue distribution model in the stablecoin industry. For investors, the question is not whether OpenUSD will replace USDC in the short term, but whether stablecoin reserve returns will shift from issuers to payment networks, exchanges, wallets, e-commerce platforms, and other distribution channels. CoinDesk reports that OpenUSD's threat to Circle lies in its challenge to Circle's business model. CoinDesk states that Circle primarily relies on interest income from retained USDC reserve assets, while OpenUSD plans to distribute this revenue to its partners. CoinDesk also cited the opinion of Rob Hadick, general partner of Dragonfly, that the list of partners for OpenUSD shows a real threat to Circle's business. CRCL fell 17.52% in a single day. Following the announcement of OpenUSD, Circle's stock price fell significantly. Market data shows that as of the close of trading on June 30th (US time), Circle Internet Group (CRCL) closed at $62.63, down approximately 17.52% from the previous trading day. The Block reported that Circle's stock price fell by more than 16% after the launch of OpenUSD. The report pointed out that investors are assessing the potential competitive pressure of OpenUSD on USDC. The Block also cited analyst William Blair's opinion, stating that market concerns about OpenUSD may be exaggerated, and Circle's first-mover advantage, liquidity, and payment infrastructure still put it in a good position. CoinDesk also reported that the emergence of OpenUSD will increase market scrutiny of Circle's economic model and its partnership with Coinbase, while also indicating that stablecoin competition is shifting towards distribution platforms such as exchanges, payment processors, and wallets. Besides the anticipated business competition brought by OpenUSD, some reports also mentioned that index adjustments may affect CRCL's short-term trading, but judging from reports by Reuters, CoinDesk, and The Block, OpenUSD remains one of the most prominent market narratives of the day. Coinbase's Role Draws Attention The inclusion of Coinbase in the Open USD participation list has also attracted market attention. Coinbase has long been a key partner and distribution channel for USDC. CoinDesk reported that Circle and Coinbase jointly established the Centre Consortium to promote USDC issuance and share economic benefits related to USDC reserve returns according to a commercial agreement. Coinbase also appears on the list of partners in the Open USD announcement, thus the market is beginning to refocus on the partnership between Circle and Coinbase. However, Coinbase's participation in Open Standard should not be directly interpreted as "abandoning USDC". A more conservative way to put it is that Coinbase's participation in the Open USD ecosystem means it may offer customers more stablecoin infrastructure options in the future. For Circle, Coinbase's significance lies in distribution and liquidity. If Coinbase supports more USD stablecoins in the future, or redistributes economic benefits in the cooperation agreement, USDC's default channel advantage may be reassessed by the market. Following the Open USD announcement, Circle co-founder and CEO Jeremy Allaire responded to the stablecoin competition. The Block reported that Allaire stated Circle welcomes competition and will continue to expand the USDC ecosystem. The Block also stated that Allaire said Circle will continue to invest in partnerships with banks, payment companies, capital market institutions, and enterprises, and enable partners to become economic stakeholders in the growth of the USDC network in more ways. This response indicates that Circle does not describe OpenUSD as a direct short-term threat, but rather treats it within the context of the stablecoin industry's expansion and increased competition. Allaire's mention of the economic benefits of enabling partners to participate more in the growth of the USDC network also illustrates that partner incentive mechanisms have become an important variable in stablecoin competition. Market opinions remain divided. Those who support the claim that OpenUSD poses a threat argue that OpenUSD has a strong lineup of partners and that its revenue-sharing mechanism directly impacts Circle's revenue model. Reuters reports that Open Standard brings together more than 140 companies; CoinDesk states that Stripe, Coinbase, Mastercard, Visa, and BlackRock are among the project's launch partners. However, some argue that the emergence of OpenUSD does not equate to the collapse of USDC. Some discussions in the Chinese market suggest that stablecoins are not simply about issuing a token, but require long-term liquidity, trading scenarios, user mindset, and channel development. While many exchanges and financial institutions have launched various stablecoins in the past, USDT and USDC are the most significant in terms of scale. Even with a strong list of partners, OpenUSD needs to demonstrate its ability to generate trading depth, payment scenarios, and sustained demand. The Block, citing analysts Andrew Jeffrey and Adib Choudhury from William Blair, states that market concerns about OpenUSD are exaggerated. Two analysts stated that competition in the stablecoin market is inevitable and validates the sector's potential; however, USDC still boasts a large market capitalization, deep liquidity, and payment infrastructure such as the Circle Payments Network, making it difficult for new entrants to replicate Circle's model. Lorenzo Valente, Research Director at ARK Invest, also expressed skepticism about OpenUSD. He believes that these consortium-based stablecoins first face the challenge of a liquidity cold start, struggling to establish trading depth close to USDC and USDT in a short period, and lacking substantial trading pairings with existing mainstream crypto markets. Valente also believes that the consortium governance structure may affect decision-making efficiency. If participants have both cooperative and competitive relationships, the project may be less flexible than a single decision-making entity like Circle and Tether in terms of incentive allocation, product adjustments, and market response. In the Chinese market discussions, another type of controversy focuses on Circle's own competitive advantages. Some argue that Circle should not be simply compared to Visa or Mastercard, whose core revenue comes from payment networks and transaction channels, while Circle's main revenue comes from the returns on USDC reserve assets. Others believe that the real challenge of stablecoins lies not just in issuance, but in acceptance, clearing, payment networks, and financial channels; banks and payment companies have a natural advantage in corporate accounts, cross-border settlements, merchant networks, and USD deposit and withdrawal channels. Still others believe that the sharp drop in CRCL's stock price and the participation of hundreds of institutions in stablecoin projects indicate that the stablecoin sector may be reshaped by regulation and traditional finance in the long term; the future landscape of stablecoins will be determined not only by the scale of issuance, but also by regulation, application scenarios, and the internal needs of the crypto ecosystem. Further observation is needed regarding actual adoption. Several key details about Open USD remain undisclosed. Reuters reports that Open USD officially plans to launch this year. The WSJ reports that Open USD is planned to be available on Base, Solana, and other networks. However, the issuing entity, compliance framework, reserve asset custody arrangements, redemption process, profit distribution details, governance weights, and specific integration methods for partners in their respective products still need clarification. For stablecoins, the list of partners is only the first step. Whether a stablecoin can achieve scale depends not only on the presence of large companies but also on sufficient exchange liquidity, wallet support, merchant settlement scenarios, DeFi integration, cross-border payment demand, and compliance approval. The advantages of USDC and USDT stem from their long-term accumulated liquidity, trading pairs, user habits, and institutional use cases. OpenUSD needs to gradually prove itself in these areas. The drop in CRCL on June 30th cannot be simply interpreted as the market having already decided that USDC will be replaced by OpenUSD. A more accurate statement is that OpenUSD is bringing Circle's core business model back to the market. Going forward, we need to pay attention to OpenUSD's issuance scale, redemption efficiency, exchange liquidity, merchant access, and on-chain usage after its launch this year, as well as whether USDC's distribution channels and institutional adoption will be affected.

data.title: Who will be most hurt when Meta starts selling computing power? What is the real signal?
data.description: On July 1st, Bloomberg reported that Meta is internally developing a project codenamed "Meta Compute," which plans to sell surplus AI computing power to external customers. Two paths are being pursued simultaneously: Managed model access—opening up models such as Llama and Muse Spark to enterprise customers, charging based on token usage, similar to AWS Bedrock; Direct sales of raw computing power—renting out GPU clusters by the hour, similar to CoreWeave. The news immediately triggered a market reaction: META rose approximately 9% that day, which investors interpreted as Zuckerberg's positive response to questions about whether the $100 billion AI investment could be recouped. Meanwhile, CoreWeave and Nebius both fell by about 15% (247 Wall St) – these two Neocloud (emerging AI cloud) companies, which rely on "selling GPU time," suddenly faced a competitor dozens of times their size. This was not accidental. As Zuckerberg stated in the May shareholder call: "This is definitely one of our options. Almost every week, external companies contact us hoping we'll launch an API service, or inquiring about purchasing our computing power, even willing to pay above our procurement costs." His full statement at the time was: "We haven't done that yet because we believe this computing power still has its own uses. However, if in the future we see a surplus of computing power, then that will be an option we can take." This statement is now becoming action. To understand why Meta is selling computing power, we first need to know how much it has built. In April 2026, Meta raised its full-year capital expenditure guidance to $125 billion to $145 billion (confirmed in the SEC 10-Q filing), almost doubling the actual capex of $72.2 billion in 2025. This figure briefly alarmed investors during the earnings call—the stock price fell 10% that day. But Zuckerberg didn't back down. His logic is: "The biggest bottleneck facing the entire industry remains the supply of computing power, so we should reserve as much computing power resources as possible first, and then decide how to utilize them in the future." How many have actually been built? A few figures: This isn't just a Meta problem. Microsoft, Google, and Amazon are also spending money like crazy. In 2026, the combined capex of the four tech giants will approach $700 billion. This money isn't buying software; it's buying steel, electricity, Nvidia GPUs, and data centers springing up everywhere. Why is there surplus computing power: the utilization gap between training and inference? There's a physics problem with computing power: it's not consumed uniformly. Training a large language model might fully utilize tens of thousands of GPUs for several months. But after training, the utilization of this cluster drops sharply to 30%–50%—only inference requests are running, and the computing power required for inference is far lower than that for training. Meta's training pacing is publicly known: Llama 4 is trained, while Llama 5 is still in development. During the interim period, the cluster simply sits there, consuming electricity without generating any returns. Zuckerberg's strategy is called "Hoard now, decide later." Build the infrastructure based on peak training demand first, and how to use it later—that's a matter for later. The premise of this strategy is that there will always be a reason to use this computing power in the future. And "selling it" is one of those reasons. From a physical perspective, this isn't a waste caused by Meta's mismanagement—it's an inherent cycle of computing infrastructure development. As long as you're doing cutting-edge AI research, you'll inevitably face this issue sooner or later. The difference with Meta is that it was the first to acknowledge this and take action. Meta isn't the first to do this. Pioneers emerge: SpaceX/xAI's computing power business. In May 2026, Musk's SpaceX/xAI completed two groundbreaking computing power leasing contracts: First: Anthropic leases Colossus 1. Monthly rent: $1.25 billion. Contract term: until 2029. Total contract value: approximately $45 billion. The first contract: All available computing power (200,000+ NVIDIA GPUs) at the Colossus 1 data center in Memphis, Tennessee. The second contract: Google leases Colossus 2. Monthly rent: $920 million. The third contract: The computing power cluster of the Colossus 2 data center. Combined, these two contracts mean SpaceX/xAI earns over $26 billion annually just from "leasing GPUs". More importantly, it validates the feasibility of the "self-built data center → selling computing power" model. Meta is following the same path. Moreover, Meta's scale is far greater than xAI—it has committed $182.9 billion to infrastructure development. Who suffers most: Neocloud's double squeeze. Meta's entry directly puts pressure on Neocloud. The business model of these companies is simple: they purchase/lease GPUs in bulk from upstream suppliers (massive buyers like Nvidia or Meta), then break them down into hourly computing power and rent it out to downstream AI startups, research institutions, and enterprise users. CoreWeave is the most well-known example—it went public in March 2025, and its market capitalization once exceeded $50 billion after its IPO. Its core assets are a large number of GPUs and a portfolio of long-term customer contracts. However, after Meta entered the market, two cracks appeared in CoreWeave's business model: Crack 1: Downstream customers were diverted. If AI startups could directly rent computing power from Meta—and Meta's GPUs were newer, larger in scale, and potentially cheaper—why would they need CoreWeave? Crack 2: The biggest customer became a competitor. CoreWeave and Meta already had a deep partnership. In April 2026, CoreWeave signed a $35 billion computing power supply agreement with Meta (until 2032), with $21 billion covering new additions from 2027 to 2032. Nebius also signed a similar $27 billion agreement with Meta. These contracts are structured with CoreWeave/Nebius supplying computing power to Meta—Meta being the buyer. If Meta decides to build its own computing power and sell it externally, it is likely to reduce its external procurement from CoreWeave and Nebius. This will reduce the future renewal rate and new additions to these contracts. The market is pricing in not only "Meta becoming a competitor," but also "Meta no longer being a reliable large customer." There's an even deeper risk: the valuation of the collateral used for financing. Neocloud's expansion is heavily reliant on debt financing, and the collateral is their GPU clusters. In March 2026, CoreWeave closed an $8.5 billion GPU-secured term loan—claimed to be the industry's first investment-grade GPU-collateralized debt (Nasdaq announcement). If hyperscale players like Meta enter the computing power leasing market on a large scale, hourly GPU rental fees will fall—the valuation of the collateral will shrink—and debt refinancing will become more difficult. This isn't a theoretical deduction. On the day the news broke, CoreWeave fell 15%. The market is repricing. A Bigger Picture: The $700 Billion Infrastructure Gamble – Who Will Run First? Let's broaden our perspective. Meta selling computing power isn't just a matter for Meta alone. In 2026, the combined capex of the four tech giants will approach $700 billion. The vast majority of this money will flow in the same direction: AI infrastructure. The question then arises: what will be the utilization rate of these infrastructure projects after they are built? The logic of the bears: GPU computing power prices continue to decline. B300 cloud instances start at around $7.4/hour on demand, and spot instances start at around $4.3/hour (GPUFinder, July 2026). More broadly, LLM inference costs have decreased by approximately 1000 times in three years (GPU Nexus). Inference efficiency is leaping. DeepSeek R1 and the latest Anthropic models are doing more with less computing power. Some analysts compare this to the over-expansion of fiber optic networks in the late 1990s—when telecom companies frantically laid fiber optic cables, resulting in oversupply, price collapse, and the bankruptcy of many companies. The bullish logic: Jevons' Paradox: the cheaper computing power, the more people use it. Not linear growth, but exponential growth. Inference demand is exploding. In 2026, inference workloads accounted for about two-thirds of all AI computing power, far higher than one-third a year earlier. The current AI penetration rate is roughly equivalent to the internet in 1995—you might think you built too much back then, but looking back, you didn't build enough. Both sides have their points. But one fact is undeniable: Meta didn't wait for "confirmed oversupply" before selling computing power. It reserved an outlet for "potential oversupply." This is the truly noteworthy signal. If you are 100% confident in your computing power needs, you don't need to plan ahead for selling computing power. Just build it. Meta has prepared backup plans. What about the others? Microsoft, Google, and Amazon—their core business is selling cloud computing power; the question of "whether or not to sell computing power" doesn't apply to them—they've always been selling it. The real issue is that they are also expanding rapidly, and at a rate no slower than Meta. If the largest buyers are all preparing backup plans—then this market might not be so complex. The First Crack in Infrastructure Investment Over the past two years, the logic behind AI infrastructure investment has been: "Demand is unlimited, and computing power will never be enough." Now, this logic is showing its first crack—not because demand has disappeared, but because the speed of supply construction may have outpaced demand. Meta's sale of computing power marks the transition of AI infrastructure construction from "building without regard to cost" to "starting to calculate costs." Several things worth observing next: Will Microsoft follow suit? It has a deep partnership with OpenAI and a large amount of Azure computing power—it doesn't need to build new infrastructure; it's already selling it. But will its expansion speed slow down? CoreWeave's next quarterly earnings report. Can it use contract data to prove it's unaffected? The trend of GPU hourly rates. If Meta officially enters the market, will a price war break out?

data.title: What are the future judicial practices for dealing with virtual currencies in my country?
data.description:
Introduction

In recent years, virtual currencies have appeared more and more frequently in criminal cases involving telecommunications fraud, online gambling, and money laundering. Criminals favor them for three main reasons: fast transfers, cross-border capabilities, and difficulty in tracing. But when the public security organs are halfway through the investigation, a practical problem arises—how should these seized virtual currencies be handled? Should they be confiscated and directly transferred to the public prosecution authorities? Should they be disposed of and converted into cash? Or should they be returned to the victims?

It sounds like a small matter, but in practice, it is fraught with danger. Yesterday, Attorney Liu saw Professor Xie Dengke's article "Practical Models and Development and Improvement of the Handling of Virtual Currency Cases" published in the "Political and Legal Forum".

Today, we'll discuss the current state of judicial handling of virtual currencies in my country and its potential future direction. Why are virtual currencies so "difficult to seize"? Traditionally, handling stolen money and goods is not complicated. For example, a stack of cash, a car, or a house can be seized by the police and placed in a special storage location or account, making it untouchable. However, virtual currency is a "new species," essentially a string of code existing on the network, which brings several challenges that traditional seizure methods cannot handle. First, it's "virtual"—you can't physically seize it. The police can seize the phone containing the currency, but the currency itself isn't on the phone. As long as someone knows the account password or possesses the "private key" (which can be understood as the unique key to unlock a door), they can still transfer the coins using a different device. Even if the suspect hands over the private key on the spot, there's no guarantee they haven't secretly backed it up elsewhere. Secondly, it's "decentralized," so freezing the account won't work. Banks have headquarters and branches; they can freeze an account with a single order. But cryptocurrencies don't have a "headquarters." They rely on thousands of computers worldwide to keep records; freezing one point won't stop its continued circulation. Unless the coin involved is centralized, such as stablecoins like USDT or USDC, or stored on a centralized exchange like Binance or Easy, then mainland law enforcement and judicial authorities can request the stablecoin issuer or cryptocurrency exchange to freeze the wallet addresses and accounts involved. Finally, it remains anonymous, making it impossible to trace who is behind it. Bitcoin's ledger only records "transfer from address A to address B," but not who A and B actually belong to. This makes it very difficult to determine whether "this coin is illicit money, or whether it belongs to him/her." Of course, with professional on-chain tracing technology, anonymity is not entirely unsolvable, but it is indeed very difficult. Furthermore, its ability to flow across borders instantly makes tracing slow and laborious—coins can be transferred abroad with a simple click, but our judicial jurisdiction cannot directly extend to other countries; we can only go through cumbersome international assistance procedures. These combined troubles are the real "roadblocks" for judicial organs when dealing with virtual currencies. Faced with the above difficulties, Professor Xie summarized two main handling models explored by law enforcement agencies in various regions. These two models are essentially just execution methods of "seizure," a form of temporary custody, and do not mean that the currency will ultimately be nationalized. The first method is called "currency transfer." Since confiscating the phone is useless, they simply transfer all the cryptocurrency in the suspect's wallet to a wallet controlled by the police. The suspect doesn't know the private key of the new wallet, so he can no longer access the cryptocurrency. Ultimately, this is the same principle as moving stolen money from the suspect's home to a special custody account—it's just a change of storage location; the nature remains the same—it's still "seizure." The second method is called "monetization and preservation." This involves selling the cryptocurrency for cash first, then confiscating the cash. Why sell? The legitimate reason is that some cryptocurrencies (like Bitcoin) experience volatile prices, and if they aren't sold soon, they might depreciate; selling in time preserves their value. However, this hides a problem of "distortion." In reality, many places sell cryptocurrencies not genuinely to prevent devaluation, but because the technical barriers to directly seizing the coins are too high, and law enforcement personnel are not proficient enough. They simply sell them off to save time and effort. In this way, the original intention of "preserving value" quietly becomes a means of "circumventing technical difficulties." For example, in the infamous PlusToken pyramid scheme case, the liquidation price of the involved coins was far lower than the market price at the time. Objectively, this resulted in the loss of state-owned assets (which, regardless of their classification, would be confiscated by the national treasury); furthermore, if compensation was involved, victims could not recover their losses in full. Therefore, there is a proper order to handling these two models: prioritize transferring what can be transferred, and avoid selling them indiscriminately. Only for coins with large price fluctuations and a genuine risk of decline can they be sold after obtaining the rights holder's consent and completing the approval procedures. Coins like USDT, which are pegged to the US dollar and have inherently stable prices, should not, in principle, be easily liquidated. This is the current situation. What truly deserves attention is how this matter might be resolved in the future. In summary, there are roughly four directions.

Is there an ideal solution?

(I) Let technology solve technical problems

Many of the problems with virtual currencies stem from technical issues—they are difficult to catch, freeze, and investigate. Therefore, the most appropriate solution should be technology. The future trend is to truly utilize increasingly mature technologies such as on-chain tracking, address identification, and fund flow analysis in case handling, improving the judicial organs' ability to "catch and manage cryptocurrencies," rather than resorting to "selling first and worrying later" to avoid technical obstacles. In other words, law enforcement agencies need to improve their technical skills, not use monetization to cover up their technical shortcomings.

This point forms the foundation for the following directions. (II) Opening a "Legal Loop" in Policy my country adopts a "prohibitory" and stringent regulation of virtual currencies—trading and speculation are prohibited within the country. The core purpose is to safeguard national financial security and monetary sovereignty. However, this leads to an awkward situation: criminals can easily convert virtual currencies into cash and leave overseas, while the police, due to policy restrictions, cannot find legal channels to dispose of the seized currency. This "unequal" situation—one side can convert, the other cannot—results in the state not being able to collect the fines and confiscations it should, and victims not being able to recover the money they are entitled to. The future approach, in its broadest sense, is likely to be: not to overturn the "Document No. 42" ban issued by eight ministries in 2026, but rather to maintain strong regulatory policies while opening a small loophole: establishing a closed, officially-led channel for handling cryptocurrency cases. This channel will only serve judicial proceedings, will not be open to the public, will not facilitate cryptocurrency trading, and will be equipped with dedicated accounts, custody, and auditing. This maintains the bottom line of "prohibiting trading" while providing necessary operational space for domestic judicial organs to handle cases legally. Internationally, some countries with similarly strict regulations are also exploring similar dedicated official platforms. (III) The Order of Judicial Disposal The aforementioned "prioritize transfers where possible" is currently more of an academic suggestion; it needs to be transformed into a clear rule in the future. The logic is simple: directly seizing the original item (i.e., transferring the currency) has the lowest cost, and if a mistake is made in the future, it can be returned in its original state; while selling it for cash incurs transaction and assessment costs, and if a mistake is made, it is difficult to recover. Therefore, under normal circumstances, "transfer" should be given priority. Only when the price of the currency has indeed fallen sharply and selling is absolutely necessary should the path of "cashing out" be taken, and it must be with the consent of the rights holder and legal approval. Only by writing this order into the rules can the loophole of "selling for the sake of convenience" be plugged from the system. (IV) The most crucial step - let the court "make the decision" If the previous points were about "how to seize the currency correctly", then this last point concerns "who has the final say after seizing the currency". A common practice nowadays is that public security organs seize property, often without a court trial, and directly confiscate it. Some scholars believe this is tantamount to the investigative organs being both player and referee—seizing and handling the property themselves, leaving the parties involved no opportunity to voice their opinions or protest their innocence. This is clearly wrong. We all know that convicting and sentencing someone requires a court trial; so why can someone's property be deprived without a trial? Attorney Liu's view is that, according to departmental regulations such as the "Several Provisions on the Management of Case-Related Property by Public Security Organs" and the "Procedures for Handling Criminal Cases by Public Security Organs," as well as the "Opinions on Further Regulating the Handling of Case-Related Property in Criminal Proceedings" issued by the General Offices of the CPC Central Committee and the State Council, public security organs can dispose of and liquidate case-related property under certain conditions. However, based on the conservative principle of criminal procedure and the principle of protecting the legitimate rights and interests of suspects, the author tends to believe that the handling of case-related property during the investigation stage must be extremely cautious, and disposal should only be implemented after exhausting all other measures. The future direction could take advantage of the opportunity presented by the amendment to the Criminal Procedure Law to establish a dedicated "procedure for the disposal of seized assets." To put it bluntly: during the investigation phase, the police can only "temporarily hold" the seized virtual currency; whether it is ultimately confiscated, refunded, or returned must be decided in court, with prosecutors presenting evidence, defense lawyers, suspects, victims, and other relevant parties all having the opportunity to speak, and finally, the court holding a public trial before making a final decision. In this way, the disposal of assets will no longer be an "internal operation" of a single agency, but a matter open to all parties. In conclusion, Attorney Liu largely agrees with Professor Xie's point: combating crime is certainly important, but the fact that the other party may be a "bad person" who could be convicted in the future does not mean that their assets can be disposed of arbitrarily and carelessly. No matter how new or technologically complex virtual currency is, it should not be used as an excuse to circumvent procedural justice. The path my country will take in the future is already quite clear: technical difficulties will be overcome with technology; policy obstacles will be cleared through a closed legal channel; the order of processing will be regulated by clear rules; and the most fundamental bottom line is that any final disposal of citizens' property must go through the courts and follow due process. This is not only being responsible to the state and the victims, but also to every ordinary person who might be involved in such cases. After all, how we treat the property of "bad guys" today largely determines whether tomorrow's system can protect the property of every ordinary person.

data.title: A New Order in the Crypto Market Under Liquidity Repricing
data.description: Recently, HTX Research, Huobi HTX's dedicated research department, released its Q3 2026 forward-looking report, "Liquidity Defines the Crypto Market: A New Crypto Market Order Under Global Liquidity Repricing." Starting with market changes that occurred in Q2, the report from HTX Research provides a comprehensive assessment of the next phase, focusing on two main themes: liquidity and regulation. [Read the full report: https://square.htx.com/zh/htx-research-quarterly-strategy-2026-q3-liquidity-defines-crypto-a-new-crypto-order-under-global-liquidity-repricing-2/] The release of this quarterly forward-looking report stems from Huobi HTX's observation of users' real needs: in an information-overloaded and narrative-driven crypto market, users need an analytical framework that can penetrate the noise and clearly identify the medium-term trends. HTX Research operates on a quarterly basis, integrating macro liquidity, policy regulation, and on-chain fundamentals into a unified framework. This helps users understand the deeper drivers of the market beyond price fluctuations, thereby clarifying trends and building their own strategy framework. Looking back at the recently concluded second quarter, HTX Research believes that the price pullback in crypto assets was essentially a global liquidity repricing driven by macro factors. The long-term logic and fundamentals of the crypto industry have not been reversed. The combined forces of the Federal Reserve's hawkish policy, a stronger dollar, a reversal in spot ETF fund flows, and a cooling of corporate treasury buying have collectively increased funding costs and compressed market risk budgets. This adjustment differs significantly from the previous bear market: the latter stemmed from a collapse in industry credit and damage to institutional trust, while this round of pressure is concentrated at the macro level, with rising funding costs and the disappearance of marginal buying being the main causes. This judgment is clearly supported by data: Bitcoin fell back to around $59,000 in June after reaching a peak of approximately $82,000 in mid-May, with the peak-to-trough valuation compressed by about 24%; the spot Bitcoin ETF saw a net outflow of nearly $4.9 billion in May and June, with previous core marginal buying turning into selling pressure. Based on this background, HTX Research makes three judgments on various assets. First, global dollar liquidity remains the determining factor for Bitcoin's medium-term trend; geopolitical conflicts such as the situation in Iran only change short-term risk appetite. In the second quarter, gold outperformed Bitcoin at one point, further confirming that its pricing logic is now closer to that of globally liquid assets. Second, the market no longer pays a premium for simple narratives; the divergence between ETH, DeFi, and altcoins indicates that ecosystem activity, TVL, and user growth can only support valuations if they are translated into transaction fees, revenue, token burning, or clear token value capture. Finally, the price pullback did not halt infrastructure expansion. The size of tokenized RWA (excluding stablecoins) rose from approximately $29.49 billion in the second quarter to $32.28 billion, with US Treasury tokenization continuing to contribute the main incremental growth. Stablecoin settlement, on-chain securities, and institutional compliance channels also progressed simultaneously. Regarding the third quarter, the report points out that market direction will depend on two yet-to-be-confirmed conditions: whether global liquidity can stop deteriorating, and whether regulatory certainty can release institutional risk budgets. Based on this, HTX Research proposes three variables that need continuous monitoring: first, whether the Federal Reserve continues its hawkish reaction function; second, the liquidity-draining effect of the US Treasury's debt issuance pace and TGA reconstruction (after the RRP buffer was largely exhausted, TGA has replaced traditional QT as a more critical liquidity variable); and third, the legislative process of the CLARITY Act. The bill has passed markup to the Senate Banking Committee by a vote of 15 to 9 and is on the legislative agenda. Whether it can cross the 60-vote threshold will be the biggest policy variable in the third quarter. Based on this, the report constructs three scenarios and their probabilities: Under the baseline scenario (60%), liquidity improves only slightly, and regulatory progress continues but has not yet been fully realized, leading to a more likely structural market recovery; the optimistic scenario (25%) corresponds to a decline in inflation, a weaker dollar, and unexpected regulatory progress; the pessimistic scenario (15%) arises from a renewed shock to energy prices and further contraction in liquidity. The report also analyzes the sensitivity of various asset classes to these two main themes. Bitcoin is considered a proxy variable for global liquidity, often reflecting marginal changes in liquidity first; RWA, supported by a high-interest-rate environment and institutional compliance requirements, is seen as a structural mainstay across cycles; the revaluation of high-quality DeFi depends more on real income, risk governance, and value capture capabilities than on TVL size; ETH's recovery awaits further confirmation from transaction fees, burning, and ETF fund flows. HTX Research believes that the market in the third quarter will not reward all risks; liquidity, cash flow, and compliance pathways will be key differentiators of asset performance. This report aims to help global Huobi HTX users clearly identify key variables and verification signals for the third quarter, providing a reference for judging market trends in a complex macroeconomic environment. In the future, HTX Research will continue to publish "Quarterly Outlook" and other high-quality investment research content, updating its analytical framework in sync with the evolution of the macroeconomic and policy environment, providing stable support for users to track market trends and refine their strategies over the long term. Note: This article does not constitute investment advice. About HTX Research HTX Research is the dedicated research department of Huobi HTX, responsible for in-depth analysis, comprehensive reporting, and professional assessments across a wide range of areas including cryptocurrencies, blockchain technology, and emerging market trends. HTX Research is committed to providing data-driven insights and strategic foresight, playing a key role in shaping industry perspectives and supporting informed decision-making in the digital asset space. With rigorous research methodologies and cutting-edge data analysis, HTX Research remains at the forefront of innovation, leading industry thought and fostering a deeper understanding of ever-changing market dynamics. Visit us. For further communication, please contact [email protected]

data.title: Tether CEO: The Dangers of the EU MiCA License
data.description: **Note:** The European Union's MiCA (MiAssurance for Crypto Assets) regulation officially came into effect on July 1st. The European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) requires crypto service providers without a MiCA license to cease or restrict their services in the EU and assist users in transferring assets or self-custody. However, many well-known crypto companies, including Tether, have failed to obtain a MiCA license. In a previous interview with Tether CEO Paolo Ardoino (linked to Cointelegraph), Ardoino revealed that Tether proactively decided not to apply for a European MiCA license and explained his reasons. (Translated by Claw, Jinse Finance) Paolo Ardoino: "As CEO, I've been told I'm 'crazy' because I decided not to apply for a MiCA license. The reason isn't fear of regulation or compliance, but rather that a MiCA license is extremely dangerous for stablecoins. I need to protect our more than 400 million users globally, who are far less fortunate than Europeans." If a regulation doesn't allow us to do this, we'll have to abandon that market—a decision every CEO must make. The reason I decided not to apply for a MiCA license is because MiCA's requirements for stablecoins are extremely dangerous, and I believe they are even more dangerous for the European banking system. In the next few years, several European banks will fail because of MiCA's requirements, which require 60% of reserves to be held in European banks as uninsured cash deposits. To give a rough estimate: assuming your stablecoin issuance is €10 billion, you'll need to hold €6 billion in multiple European banks. However, large banks (like UBS) don't provide banking services to stablecoin companies, so you'll have to turn to smaller European banks—at least five. And each bank has deposit limits, so you must find five small European banks to hold that €6 billion. The result is that this €6 billion goes into these banks as uninsured cash deposits, which the banks can then lend out because their fractional reserve ratio is only 10%—meaning €5.4 billion will be lent to people who want to buy cars, houses, or start businesses, leaving the banks with only €600 million. Assuming you face a 20% redemption demand, that would require €2 billion. If you go to the bank to withdraw, and the bank only has €600 million, it will go bankrupt because it cannot meet your demand, and you, as the issuer of the stablecoin, will also go bankrupt. Therefore, I believe this is poorly considered legislation. I support legislation, but legislation should be wise and carefully designed to protect consumers. Therefore, I decided not to apply for a MiCA license because I must protect our more than 400 million users worldwide—who are not as fortunate as Europeans. I love Europe, but unfortunately, I think the European Central Bank (ECB) is more interested in pushing a digital euro to control people and their spending habits than in genuinely caring about the best interests of its citizens. We have hundreds of millions of people outside Europe who desperately need a payment system because the existing system in their lives—based on their national currencies—is utterly useless because those currencies depreciate so rapidly against the dollar, Bitcoin, gold, and other assets. So I must stay true to my principles and protect these people, even from poor regulation. Of course, there are different euro stablecoin products on the market. We've invested in grassroots euro stablecoins through Stellar. But the ECB is determined to push for euro-based stablecoins. I remember the ECB once published an article or press release expressing its fear of dollar-backed stablecoins. Interestingly, they blame US stablecoins as if they were the main reason for the euro's failure—it's somewhat laughable that they don't reflect on their own practices but instead point the finger at dollar stablecoins. However, European exchanges should be able to do well; we're supporting them with Hadron (our tokenization technology) to provide them with alternatives based on European grassroots stablecoins. I'm a Bitcoin believer. I believe it's crucial to protect people's right to hold strong assets that are carefully designed to be as safe as possible. This is my concern about the MiCA stablecoin terms. I know competitors are deciding to apply for MiCA, but it was these very competitors who nearly failed in 2023 when Silicon Valley Bank had $3 billion in uninsured cash deposits, and they were only saved by the FDIC. That's not a good example of compliance. Compliance is very important, but only if regulation is reasonable. Otherwise, frankly, very few companies—sorry for being blunt—have the guts to abandon a market of 600 million people to cater to 300 million people who have no other choice. The Europeans aren't to blame, but European regulators haven't truly grasped the problem and how to properly address it. In fact, I've participated in numerous discussions on behalf of Tether advocating for 100% reserves to be held in government bonds (such as short-term bonds). On the other hand, our competitors are pushing for 100% cash holdings—they clearly haven't learned from their mistakes. Returning to dollar stablecoins, we aim to maintain the highest quality assets in Tether's reserves. If you're a dollar stablecoin, you should be able to hold short-term U.S. Treasury bonds, which can be quickly converted into cash. This is what Tether users expect of us, and that's exactly what we do. If a regulation doesn't allow us to do this—like this European regulation—then we have to abandon that market. We are good friends with Rumble (a media streaming platform). They faced demands from France to block and remove certain content creators—they decided not to serve the French market and shut down their operations there. As a company, you have to decide whether to protect freedom of speech and financial freedom while complying with legitimate requests and law enforcement demands—Tether has never refused legitimate requests from law enforcement agencies, and that must be clear. But we would rather give up a portion of our customer base if it means our entire user base will be affected and our product weakened. I am very firm and very cautious on this point. (Regarding Bitcoin) I think this is inevitable. Companies and countries must buy Bitcoin. I see central banks in BRICS countries accumulating large amounts of gold, but this is just what they've been doing for decades, in their comfort zone, and individuals and companies are doing the same. But I think in the medium to long term, the more Bitcoin education there is, the more companies will follow the pioneers (those who were the first to put Bitcoin in their vaults), and then everyone else will follow. And it's never too late to buy Bitcoin.

data.title: On-chain US stocks become a new variable in China's cross-border capital regulation.
data.description:
A couple of days ago, I discussed China's regulation of cross-border investment with a friend from a mainland government department. They raised a very direct question: With cross-border brokerages like Futu, Tiger Brokers, and Changqiao Securities being restricted, will on-chain US stocks on cryptocurrency exchanges become a new channel for capital outflow from mainland China? The regulatory authorities are very perceptive. In the past few years, many mainland users bought Hong Kong and US stocks through cross-border brokerages like Futu, Tiger Brokers, and Changqiao Securities. However, this didn't last long. On May 22, 2026, the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) disclosed that it had launched an investigation into the illegal cross-border business activities of Tiger Brokers, Futu Securities, and Changqiao Securities, and issued a prior notice of administrative penalties. On the same day, eight departments, including the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC), released a rectification plan, bringing the activities of overseas institutions in China, including marketing and solicitation, account opening, processing of trading orders, fund transfers, customer service, software operation, and social media traffic generation, under regulatory scrutiny. The administrative penalties have been effective. As of June 12, the penalized overseas securities firms have largely begun to implement the regulatory requirements and cease providing securities investment services to existing clients in mainland China. Please pay close attention to the order of my statements; if you are a mainland user of the aforementioned three securities firms, you should understand the true meaning of this sentence. The story of Yu the Great controlling the floods tells us that sometimes channeling is more effective than blocking. The tightening of regulations does not mean that the demand for buying US stocks will disappear. For some mainland Chinese investors, Nvidia, Tesla, Apple, and Nasdaq ETFs (exchange-traded funds) are not just distant stock codes, but a way for them to understand global asset allocation, exchange rate risk, and wealth security. Now that the old channels are gone, they will naturally look for new entry points. For cryptocurrency exchanges, this is undoubtedly an opportunity for immense wealth and business. Futu Securities has a successor. According to disclosures by QQ, the world's largest cryptocurrency exchange, its stock trading products have seen total trading volume exceed $3 billion and assets under management surpass $1 billion since their launch on June 1, 2026, with an average daily net inflow of approximately $42 million. About one in seven page visitors registers an account, and nearly 90% of these new registered users eventually make a transaction.

The reason why blockchain-based US stocks have been able to flourish in a short period of time is not because the concept is new, but because it has optimized the previously overly cumbersome process of cross-border securities asset investment to an extremely smooth level.

According to data disclosed by a certain company, only about 11% of adults worldwide hold brokerage accounts. While US stocks account for about half of the global stock market capitalization, overseas investors hold only about 18% of that. On the company's stock trading platform, approximately 73% of stock traders come from emerging markets. This means that on-chain US stocks may not be targeting established investors accustomed to using IB and Futu, but rather a large number of users previously excluded by the service radius of traditional brokerages. A user who already holds USDT or USDC, and who has completed KYC (Know Your Customer) verification at an exchange, no longer needs to open an overseas brokerage account, wait for bank deposits and withdrawals, or worry about US stock trading hours. They only need to switch to the "Stocks" page on the exchange or connect to an on-chain stock protocol in their wallet to see the trading interface for US stocks and ETFs. Some exchanges have even begun supporting securities transfers, meaning that users who no longer wish to use traditional brokerage products can transfer their past stock holdings to the cryptocurrency exchange system. Through authorization and minting mechanisms, these holdings can be converted into stock tokens for 24/7 trading, collateralized lending, or entry into DeFi (Decentralized Finance). Cryptocurrency exchanges used to compete for coin trading, contracts, wealth management, and new projects; now they compete for users' global asset accounts. Stablecoins, US stocks, ETFs, gold, government bonds, Bitcoin, and Ethereum can all be crammed into a single interface. Users see an asset management portal, while regulators see an increasingly difficult-to-identify cross-border investment path based on traditional securities account logic.

Several Ways to Play On-Chain US Stocks

It should be noted that although all on-chain US stock platforms currently claim to offer this service, the solutions offered by different platforms are not consistent.

The first type is the brokerage portal type. For example, the US stock and ETF trading portals mentioned in a certain company's announcement essentially connect the exchange's front end to the traditional securities infrastructure. Users see the stock portal in their crypto accounts, but order execution, clearing, settlement, and custody still rely on brokerage firms, clearing brokers, and custody systems.

The key to this model is not whether stocks are on-chain, but rather that crypto exchanges embed traditional brokerage capabilities into their user interface.

The second type is the on-chain token type.

Products like bStocks and xStocks typically emphasize 1:1 underlying stock backing, proof of reserves, on-chain transfers, and wallet self-custody. They are more like turning US stock exposure into tradable on-chain certificates. The user interface shows stock ticker symbols, but what investors actually hold is often not the listed company's stock itself, but rather certificates or tokens issued by an offshore entity. The third type is the distribution network type. Platforms like OKX and MEXC connect to on-chain asset issuers like Ondo, with exchanges primarily serving as entry points, providing liquidity, and packaging for the user experience. What truly determines investor rights are still the issuance documents, redemption arrangements, eligibility restrictions, and platform terms. What users see is a trading pair, but the backend may contain tokenized securities, tracking certificates, or simply some kind of price exposure. The fourth type is the contract exposure type. Coinbase has launched perpetual stock contracts for eligible users outside the US, settled in USDC. Users trade synthetic exposure to US stock prices. This is further removed from "holding stocks"; it's more like turning US stock prices into a new instrument on the exchange that allows for long and short positions, leverage, and funding fees. Looking at several routes together will make it more intuitive: [Image of a figure] ... Although their approaches differ, they all achieve the same result: they are all bringing the demand for US stocks from the traditional securities market to the usage habits of crypto exchanges, stablecoin accounts, and on-chain wallets. To make this clearer, Attorney Honglin will use a certain company's on-chain US stock product as an example to explain the current state of the industry. Let's first look at the stock trading portal of a certain company. According to the disclosure of a certain company, its stock trading product was launched on June 1, 2026. Users can directly access more than 7,000 US stocks and ETFs within the company's app and complete settlements using stablecoins. This isn't the traditional experience of "re-downloading a brokerage app," but rather placing US stocks alongside the user's existing crypto accounts. The user's USDT, USDC, BNB, and crypto assets are all within the same system, as is the stock trading portal. From a legal perspective, this approach doesn't mean that APP (the company behind APP) directly handles securities custody. APP's related materials clearly state that Nest Trading Limited, acting as the introducing broker, routes securities orders to the clearing broker Alpaca Securities LLC, which handles execution, clearing, settlement, and custody; APP itself does not process or custody user securities. This arrangement seems "respectable" because it doesn't completely discard traditional securities infrastructure but rather places the brokerage and clearing capabilities in the background. However, from the perspective of Chinese regulators, this is precisely the problem. Users see a certain company's platform on the front end, using stablecoins, with access through an exchange account. Product promotions and community discussions are also likely to occur within a crypto context. As for who executes, clears, and custodies the back end, ordinary users may not really care. If regulators continue to identify these services solely based on whether "overseas brokerage apps operate in mainland China," they will miss these securities services that are repackaged by exchange interfaces. Now let's look at bStocks. According to a certain company's announcement on June 11, 2026, bStocks were issued by BTECH Holdings Limited, an affiliate of the company. The announcement positioned bStocks as a certificate representing a specific financial instrument, not the shares of the related listed company itself. Each unit of bStocks is backed 1:1 by US stocks held by a regulated custodian, and can be traded 24/7 on the spot market. Stocks and bStocks can be exchanged 1:1, and publicly verifiable proof of reserves is supported. Withdrawals can also be made to compatible BNB smart chain wallets for self-custody. The legal boundaries of bStocks cannot be ignored. An announcement stated that bStocks is among the first tokenized securities included in the official listing list of the Abu Dhabi Financial Services Authority (FSRA) under the ADGM (Abu Dhabi Financial Centre). It was issued according to the FSRA-approved prospectus and traded on an approved investment trading platform. ADGM here refers to the Abu Dhabi Financial Centre. The announcement also emphasizes that bStocks are not stocks or shares, and holders do not directly own shares or stock of the relevant listed companies; the product is only offered to qualified users in approved jurisdictions through the secondary market, not within the United States or to U.S. persons, and not publicly offered outside of ADGM. This is what makes tokenized securities so interesting. They have a compliant package and meticulously detail the issuing entity, prospectus, qualified users, jurisdictional restrictions, risk warnings, and product terms. However, for regulators in mainland China, the real concern is not whether restrictions are explicitly stated in the documents, but whether the product actually circumvents these restrictions. For example, a mainland Chinese user might buy USDT via OTC, then access an exchange, see the bStocks US stock trading portal, follow a Chinese community tutorial to complete the purchase, and even withdraw the tokens to their on-chain wallet to continue participating in DeFi. The boundaries outlined in the documentation and the real-world usage paths may not always overlap. More notably, bStocks was subsequently integrated into a certain instant exchange's dollar-cost averaging (DCA) scenario. According to the product description, eligible users can set up a bStocks DCA, automatically buying supported bStocks from a minimum of 0.01 USDC equivalent at a set interval. This design advances on-chain US stocks from "trading when you want to buy" to "continuous investment like a fund's DCA." If 24-hour trading solves the trading time problem, and fractional shares and low barriers to entry solve the purchase threshold problem, then DCA solves the continuous allocation problem. It transforms on-chain US stocks from just a new trading pair into a long-term entry point for users' savings and asset allocation habits. Regarding the market outlook for on-chain US stocks, [Company Name] is confident. [Company Name]'s research department predicts that by 2031, platforms like cryptocurrency exchanges could potentially inject $2 trillion in incremental funds into the global stock market and bring in 300 million new investors; at the current growth rate, [Company Name]'s own stock trading asset management scale could exceed $10 billion by the end of 2026. What are the regulatory challenges in mainland China? Why do on-chain US stocks pose a challenge for mainland regulators? From a Chinese regulatory perspective, the real concern is whether non-compliant funds are flowing into overseas capital markets through new product entry points. While this path has been somewhat murky in the past, regulators at least knew where to look: banks, cross-border securities firms, tax information exchange, and the domestic user-facing promotion and service chain. Simply put, there were three relatively clear checkpoints in the past. The first was the banks. When users purchased foreign exchange, remitted money, or deposited and withdrew funds, their bank accounts would leave transaction records, allowing for the identification of fund usage, counterparties, and abnormal frequency. At the regulatory and enforcement level, domestic banks would typically cooperate in inquiries, freezing, stopping payments, and retrieving transaction records. This was the most important infrastructure for traditional fund supervision. The second was securities firms. To buy Hong Kong and US stocks, users typically need to open a securities account, fill in identity and tax information, and have trading instructions, asset balances, and customer service records. Even for overseas brokerages, if they conduct account opening links, marketing, Chinese customer service, trading instruction processing, and existing customer service in mainland China, regulators can use these actions to determine whether they are illegally conducting cross-border business. The third layer is CRS, or the Financial Account Tax Information Service. It's not foolproof, and not all overseas accounts are visible to Chinese regulators in real time. However, within traditional banking, brokerage, and some financial account systems, the identity of Chinese tax residents, account balances, and some income information can theoretically be exchanged through the CRS mechanism. In other words, even if traditional cross-border securities investment involves a roundabout process, regulators and tax authorities still have the opportunity to see some traces at the account level. The problem with on-chain US stocks is that it's not simply a matter of changing one path to another; it breaks down these three gates. First, the RMB is converted into USDT or USDC through OTC, C2C, or other over-the-counter methods. What banks see might only be domestic personal transfers or a series of fund flows that don't seem directly related to securities investment. Once stablecoins enter overseas exchanges or on-chain wallets, subsequent actions no longer go through the traditional banking account system. Next, users buying on offshore exchanges could be access to stock trading, tokenized securities, tracking certificates, or even stock contracts. For users, this is "one-click buying of US stocks"; but for Chinese regulators, the chain that was previously pieced together through bank fund usage, brokerage accounts, trading instructions, and CRS information exchange has suddenly been broken. The chain of on-chain US stocks under Chinese regulatory perspective can be understood as shown in the following diagram: [figure] [figcaption] The first difficulty is that the traceability of bank funds has been weakened. In the past, regulating cross-border securities investment could be traced along the lines of bank foreign exchange purchases, cross-border remittances, securities accounts, and brokerage channels. Now, funds may first be converted into stablecoins domestically before entering overseas exchanges or wallets. Banks can see the first half, but may not know that this money ultimately becomes exposure to Nvidia, Tesla, or Nasdaq ETFs. More problematic is that over-the-counter stablecoin trading is often mixed with fraudulent funds, underground banks, and money laundering networks. Regulators are no longer tracking a single, clear cross-border remittance, but rather a chain of relationships between "domestic transfers, stablecoin transfers, exchange accounts, on-chain addresses, and securities exposures." Each segment may have someone responsible, but no single institution naturally controls the entire chain. The second challenge is that the CRS (China Revenue Standard) system for exchanging tax information is insufficient. In traditional financial accounts, a user's bank account, securities account, account balance, and investment income may fall within the scope of CRS information exchange. Even if it cannot be blocked in real time, it may form clues for tax and asset information tracking afterward. However, if on-chain US stocks occur in offshore cryptocurrency exchange accounts or on-chain wallets, the situation is much more complicated. Users hold exchange account balances, stablecoins, stock tokens, contract rights, or some kind of offshore certificate, which may not be automatically included in the CRS information exchange chain that China can effectively access, unlike traditional bank and brokerage financial accounts. This leads to a very real regulatory gap: In the traditional brokerage path, what a user buys, where their account is located, their balance, and their returns can theoretically still be seen through the financial account information exchange and tax reporting system; however, in the offshore exchange path, a user may already have exposure to overseas stocks, which mainland regulators may not be able to see in a timely manner through existing mechanisms like CRS. The third difficulty is that cooperation in investigations and law enforcement is no longer as controllable as in the banking system. In the domestic banking system, regulatory departments, public security organs, and tax authorities have relatively mature cooperation mechanisms for checking transaction records, investigating accounts, and freezing funds. Banks know who they are dealing with and what the consequences of non-cooperation will be. But offshore exchanges are different. It might be registered in an offshore jurisdiction, its product documents might state that it's not targeting restricted regions, and its front-end might not directly acknowledge serving mainland Chinese users. If mainland regulators want to investigate a user's stock exposure on an exchange, stablecoin flows, on-chain withdrawal addresses, dollar-cost averaging (DCA) records, and redemption records, whether they can obtain the information, how long it takes, and how much they can obtain all depend on the platform's jurisdiction, its compliance stance, and the efficiency of cross-border law enforcement cooperation. This has a significant impact on Chinese regulators. Previously, regulators faced a combination of "domestic banks + overseas brokers + CRS"; now they may face a combination of "domestic OTC + stablecoins + offshore exchanges + on-chain wallets + stock tokens." The former at least has relatively clear accounts and targets for investigation, while the latter breaks down the flow of funds, assets, and information into multiple different systems. This is what truly gives mainland regulators a headache regarding on-chain US stocks. It's not simply about adding another investment tool; it's about replacing the previous regulatory bottlenecks of banks, securities firms, and CRS information chains with a new chain comprised of stablecoins, offshore exchanges, and on-chain addresses. Regulation isn't completely invisible, but the cost of seeing it, the difficulty of identification, and the scope of investigation have all been significantly increased. Friendly Reminder: For investors, don't rush to treat on-chain US stocks as a Futu alternative. What you pay with stablecoins is real money, but what you receive back may not be real stocks. First, examine the exchange itself. When overseas exchanges claim compliance, they usually mean they have a license, registration, disclosure, or compliance arrangements in a specific overseas jurisdiction; this doesn't mean they can offer securities investment services to mainland Chinese residents. Especially for smaller exchanges, which lack mature asset custody, clear issuance documents, and sufficient liquidity, users may not even know who to hold responsible if they encounter delisting, suspension of redemptions, or market-making disruptions. For ordinary investors, the smaller the platform, the higher the advertised returns, and the more it emphasizes "can be bought domestically," the more to avoid. Secondly, consider your deposit and withdrawal methods. While buying stocks through cross-border brokers was cumbersome, users typically didn't need to repeatedly buy and sell cryptocurrencies in the C2C market. On-chain US stocks are different; many users need to first convert RMB to USDT before transferring stablecoins to exchanges or wallets. During this process, if you encounter funds involved in fraud, money laundering, or underground banking, your bank card may be frozen at best, and you may be involved in money laundering, fraud, or other criminal risks at worst. Many people think they are just buying US stocks, but they may be unknowingly participating in cross-border gray and black market money laundering networks. Third, you need to understand what you are actually buying. As mentioned earlier, different exchanges offer different stock token solutions. Some connect to clearing brokers, some are tracking certificates issued by offshore entities, some are synthetic exposures, and some may simply be internal accounting entries of the exchange. Just because the interface says Nvidia or Tesla doesn't mean you necessarily own shares in those companies. You need to check who holds the underlying assets, who is in custody, who can redeem them, and, if regional restrictions change, which document and whom you should use to assert your rights. For entrepreneurs starting businesses in the US stock or securities asset tokenization sector, what's truly worthwhile isn't helping users bypass brokerages. If a team's business model involves providing account opening links, commission-based agency services, community tutorials, and deposit guidance in the Chinese market, or even using phrases like "If Futu is no longer available, come here," "A new way to buy US stocks in China," or "Buy Nvidia with USDT with one click" as conversion tactics, then it's not far from the regulatory red line. Whether you call it RWA (Real-World Asset Tokenization), stock tokenization, or global asset tokenization, as long as it targets mainland Chinese users for securities asset sales, deposit/withdrawal assistance, platform account opening, and trading guidance, the risk is very high. The real potential lies in developing products within overseas compliance frameworks and building infrastructure under clearly defined licensing, investor suitability, asset custody, information disclosure, and anti-money laundering requirements. On-chain US stocks won't disappear simply because of regulatory concerns. Account opening experience, trading hours, fractional share trading, cross-border settlement, and stablecoin payments are all real issues with commercial value. However, for Chinese regulators, it's not just about adding a stock page to a few exchanges; it could become a new problem related to cross-border securities investment demand and illegal cross-border capital flows. The bigger the storm, the more expensive the fish may be. But for mainland investors and Chinese market service providers, the more realistic questions are: who owns this fish, where is the hook, and who ultimately bears the risk?
